description: Apollo 13 Flight Journal - Day 6, part 2: The Reactivation Checklist

tokens: In just a little over 17 hours, the Command Module of Apollo 13 is to splash down into the Pacific Ocean.

While the crew has been performing various tasks to get them ready for their return home, the most important of them remains undone - the plan on how to power up Odyssey in a manner that will have all crucial systems running, and keeping them powered on the batteries only.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Jim, it's time for readings on the battery charging again.
Request the Bat A voltage and the charger amps.
transcript.speaker: Brand

tokens: We have no more water in the potable tank.
We tried to get some more out a few minutes ago, and there isn't any.
transcript.speaker: Lovell

tokens: Understand you ran out of water in the CM potable tank.
transcript.speaker: Brand

tokens: Post-flight inspection found out that there was a considerable amount of water left in the potable water tank.
It is likely that either the tank itself or the water pipes were frozen at this point, preventing the crew from getting more water out of it.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: This is Apollo Control at 125 hours, 24 minutes.
We have an update now on the Lunar Module consumables lifetime.
These figures include powering up the LM at 136 hours, 15 minutes Elapsed Time.
Including that power-up, we have enough water for 30.7 more hours.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Jim, we - you were able to get those voltage - Bat A voltage and current readings?
transcript.speaker: Brand

tokens: They are still monitoring the battery charge by having one of the crew read out the values from the onboard display and reading it down to the crew.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: And we're ready to read you the first checklist installment.
This will - What we're going to readup is going to be a LM time line, a CSM time line, and checklist changes to conform with these.
And right now I have the first installment of the CSM time line ready to read up.
transcript.speaker: Brand

tokens: I'm going to get - Vance, I'm going to get Jack on the line for that; and so stand by.
transcript.speaker: Lovell

tokens: Jack Swigert is naturally the person for the job.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: And he'll need a lot of paper.
transcript.speaker: Brand

tokens: Time remaining on the electrical power at 22½ hours, which would bring us to an Elapsed Time of 100...
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: ...would bring us to an Elapsed Time on the electrical power of 147 hours, 30 minutes.
The water remaining would bring us to Elapsed Time of 155 hours, 42 minutes.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: I'm on and ready to copy.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: We want to get one into the hands of Flight and EECOM, and it'll take about a minute or 2.
Sorry to wake you up for this, but take about a minute, and then we'll read it up to you.
transcript.speaker: Brand

tokens: There has been another delay, this time to produce enough copies of the checklist for everyone who desires one in Mission Control.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: And we have about 137 hours worth of lithium hydroxide left.
Partial pressure of carbon dioxide in the Lunar Module now, is at 3 tenths of a millimeter of mercury.
The 146 hours of oxygen left in the Lunar Module would bring us to an Elapsed Time of 271 hours.
We're at 17 hours, 11 minutes away from entry into the Earth's atmosphere.
Jim Lovell reported a short time ago, that the potable water tank in the Command Module has gone dry.
There is enough of a pad in Lunar Module water, though that the crew would not have to go thirsty.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Do you have any of that CM water - bagged water, left?
transcript.speaker: Brand

tokens: I went up and tried to repressurize the surge tank and get another shot of water, and was able to repressurize the surge tank okay, but there was no water that came out of the water tank.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: We understand that there isn't any more in the potable tank, but we understood that you had put some water from that tank into bags, and I wondered if any of the bags were left.
transcript.speaker: Brand

tokens: Running out of drinking water is going to add to the crew's severe discomfort, and will lead to dehydration.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: You're going to re - get LM PADs and CSM PADs, and I don't know if you want to copy them or not.
This is something I can just read off to you.
Without you copying unless you especially want it.
transcript.speaker: Brand

tokens: Go ahead, read which ones we're going to get.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: I'll read them fast first here so you get a general idea.
LM PAD, Service Module Jett attitude, Moon viewing attitude, Sun viewing attitude, and LM Jett attitude.
The CM PADs are coarse align angles for LM attitudes during Moon and Sun sightings, coarse align angles for entry REFSMMAT, and CMC angles on entry REFSMMAT for LM Jett.
If you want, I'll read it slowly so you can copy it all down; otherwise, I'll go ahead.
transcript.speaker: Brand

tokens: These refer to information on the various attitudes they will take during the last hours of the mission to accomplish various crucial maneuvers and procedures, including leaving the Service Module behind and rotating the spacecraft stack around to make their navigational observations.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: I got - I was trying to copy it as you were reading it, Vance.
The LM PAD are Service Module jettison attitude, Moon viewing attitude, Sun viewing attitude, and LM jettison attitude.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: I'll read the CM PADs now, slowly.
Coarse align angles for LM attitudes during Moon and Sun sightings.
transcript.speaker: Brand

tokens: The plan is to use the LM to point the Command Module's optics at the Moon and the Sun to acquire the coarse align angles for the inertial platform.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: The next coarse align angles for entry REFSMMAT.
The next, CMC angles on entry REFSMMAT for (1) LM Jett, (2) Moon viewing, (3) entry.
I'll stand by until you read that back.
transcript.speaker: Brand

tokens: For the last series of maneuvers, the IMU will be aligned to the Entry REFSMMAT.
This puts their X-axis (the roll axis) along their direction of flight, with the Z-axis on the negative radius.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: The CSM pads will be coarse align angles for LM attitudes during Moon and Sun sightings.
Coarse align angles for entry REFSMMAT.
CMC - CMC angles on entry REFSMMAT for (1) LM Jettison, (2) Moon viewing, (3) entry.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: All the hordes of people that devised this procedure are going to be coming into the room in a minute, and they'd like to hold up until everybody can listen in.
transcript.speaker: Brand

tokens: We lost you there briefly while you were in an attitude where we couldn't receive you.
Jack, we'd like to hold off for about 5 minutes.

We have some more people coming in to listen to this, and it took a lot of people to devise this procedure, and a few people have been testing it out, so we'd like to have them all on hand while we give you the rest.
transcript.speaker: Brand

tokens: How's the temperature up there, Jack?
You guys chopping wood to keep warm?
transcript.speaker: Slayton

tokens: Deke, it's about 51, I think, or 50 in the LM, and it's about - I don't know - 45 or little bit less in the Command Module.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: Deke Slayton has popped onto the air to ground circuit to distract the crew with banter for the moment as they wait for the procedure reading to continue.
The topic of the LM's 10-degrees Celsius temperature is as good as any.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Oh, it's a nice fall day, huh?
transcript.speaker: Slayton

tokens: Yes, I tell you, we don't have to worry about chilldown.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: While we're waiting, do you have there also what I can expect ground to uplink me?
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: We'll have that, Jack, but - Stand by for that.
transcript.speaker: Brand

tokens: Jack, probably - probably the thing to do is to give you all of the checklist, and it will include things like this.
Then we'll have a big question and answer session afterwards, if that's okay by you.
transcript.speaker: Brand

tokens: While we're waiting, just for your information, the Command Module has been stowed per your checklist with the exception of the two Hasselblad cameras.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: And the LM is pretty well stowed with the exception when we have yet to bring in the probe and the drogue.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: The probe and the drogue are large items and would take up their precious living space in the LM.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: That's Deke Slayton talking to Jack Swigert.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Relative to your water situation, Jack, we're going to have a pretty good hack on the LM here in the next few hours, and we anticipate letting you fill your own tanks before we transfer over, so you should be in reasonably good shape there.
transcript.speaker: Slayton

tokens: We have a number of juice bags all cut and ready to go, and we tried to get the water out of the Command Module about 20 minutes ago and found out that the Command Module's potable tank was empty.
So we have no bags made up now, so we're dependent on LM water, PLSS water.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: If you feel like trying the PLSS, that's all excess, and you might want to try that at any time.
I'm sure it'll taste like PLSS but you might as well try it.
transcript.speaker: Slayton

tokens: According to the to Flight Operations Report, it was as early as 97 hours GET that Mission Control was in discussion on what the crew might drink should the CM water run out.
Medical clearance was given to drink the cooling water from the PLSS backpacks, should that become necessary.
The backpacks contained 8.3 pounds of water (some 3.4 liters) for a total of 15 pounds or 6.8 liters or water.

Alternative supplies considered were the Command Module Survival kit's water canisters with another 15 pounds (6.8 liters) of water in them, and even the water in the tubing inside the Liquid Cooling Garments, which would be 1.5 pounds, or .7 liters of water.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: All the windows in the Command Module are heavily coated with water right now.
So I don't know what kind of pictures we'll get out of them, but I'm going to try and clean them off and do the best I can with the 250-millimeter lens on the Hasselblad.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: That's why we have the top priority stuff in the LM.
We figure you've got a much better chance of getting it through there.
transcript.speaker: Slayton

tokens: Are you ready to send it up, Vance?
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: Jack, before we start reading the procedure, we'd like - We find that Bat A is topped off and in great shape.
Like to switch over and top off Bat B some more.
Give it 2 more hours of charging.
Have about a seven- or eight-step procedure here to give you, if you want to copy that.
We're switching before we start recording the entry procedures.
transcript.speaker: Brand

tokens: Stand by and let me get out my other checklist.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: Panel 3: switch Battery Charge to Off.
Panel 5; Main Bus Tie Bat A/C to Bat A/C; CB Battery Charger, Bat A Charge to open; CB Battery Charger, Bat B Charge, Close.
Next, panel 250; CB Bat B Power Entry/Postlanding to Close.
Panel 5; Main Bus Tie, Bat B/C, off.
Jack, advise that we have a backout procedure for this, but we'll read that up to you later, in about 2 hours.
And, also, that this procedure is a Delta to your - the first battery charging procedure that we gave you.
In other words, it's a Delta from where you are right now.
transcript.speaker: Brand

tokens: This is the procedure for charging Bat Baker.
Panel 3; Bat Charge, Off; panel 5; Main Bus Tie, Bat A/C to A/C; CB Bat Charger, Bat A Charge, open; CB Bat Charger, Bat B Charge, Close.
Panel 250; CB Bat B Power Entry and Postlanding, Close.
Panel 5; Main Bus Tie, Bat B/C, to Off; Panel 3; Bat Charge to B.
This is a Delta for the first battery charging procedure; you'll give me the backout procedure in a couple hours.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: This configuration removes Battery A from the line by pulling its circuit breakers, and then connecting Battery B to the power bus so that it can be charged using the LM umbilical power.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: I'll put it in work right now, then come back and stand by to copy the rest of - well, I - not the rest - everything.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: You'd never believe how much this procedure's been massaged in the last day.
transcript.speaker: Brand

tokens: This is Apollo Control at 126 hours.
Apollo 13 is 89,401 nautical miles from Earth.
Jack Swigert's in the process of discontinuing the charge on Battery A in the Command Module.
And then they will hook up to Batt - the Command Module Battery B and will charge that for about 2 hours top it off.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Distance to go is 165,571 km, speed, 1,923 m/s.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: I put Bat Baker on charge; I don't think it quite had stabilized.
It was reading 1.1 amps, 39.6 volts when I left up there.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: I guess now is as good a time to start as any.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: You're kind of weak, but readable.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: Jack, we're going to need readings on the Bat volt and charger amps about every 10 or 15 minutes for a while, so is there somebody else that can be reading them out while you're copying?
transcript.speaker: Brand

tokens: Jim will take a run up there.
We're trying to get some sleep here, and Jim'll run up and take a look at voltage and current whenever you call.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: Vance, we've got to realize that we've got to establish a work-rest cycle up here, so we just can't wait around here to just read procedures all the time up to the burn.
We've got to get them up here, look at them, and then we've got to get the people to sleep.
So take that into consideration when you get ready to uh, to send up the PADs.
transcript.speaker: Lovell

tokens: We - we should be ready to go in about 5 minutes.
transcript.speaker: Brand

tokens: Jim is starting to sound bothered by the constant delays to the promised procedures.
He makes a valid point by noting that the crew needs time to familarize themselves with the procedures, besides getting some much needed rest before the time comes to enact them.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: The regular chore of checking up the battery charge readouts continues.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: And, might as well bring the Comm up to a higher fidelity for this long period of checklist reading.
That requires, panel 16: CB Primary S-Band Power Amp, Closed.
transcript.speaker: Brand

tokens: Okay, Primary S-Band Power Amp closed now.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: And next step, panel 12: S-Band Function to Voice and Ranging Function to Ranging.
transcript.speaker: Brand

tokens: The S-Band Function is at Voice and the Ranging switch was at Ranging.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: That makes it so we can hear you better, Jack.
transcript.speaker: Brand

tokens: The LM's radio system is once again configured to work on the primary electronics and with the power amplifier on to support a higher signal strength and hence a better sound quality for the careful task of writing down the checklists.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Can we - Since the first part of it, you're just going to read and I'm going to copy and we're going to have a question and answer period later, can we get started?
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: He's a local expert on this now, so we'll turn you over to him and he'll read it off.
transcript.speaker: Brand

tokens: The implication is that there was a single hard copy of the checklist which was then taken out of Mission Control to be copied for everyone who wanted one.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: I'll give you volts and amps.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: Ken Mattingly will read the procedures.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Ken Mattingly has returned to Mission Control, this time to bring in the plan on how to revive Odyssey and bring his crewmates home.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Let me take it from the top here.
Just - there might be some overlap, but to make sure we're all on the same signal.
We're starting off with a set of time line procedures that are going to give us - end up with the normal entry checklist.
There will be some checklist changes into the book, but the bulk of what I have for you is the time line stuff.
And it starts at 6 hours and 30 minutes prior to EI and assumes that we're getting LM power to Main B in the Command Module.
I think you already have the numbers for the LM PADs and the Command Module PADs; I mean the types of PADs we'll be giving you.
And if you have a question on what they'll be doing or what they're for, I can go over those now or when we get through.
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: Yes, I'll make a little note there, and we'll discuss it when we get everything copied, huh?
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: Reproductions of the checklist can be accessed through the links throughout this chapter.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: And the first item, then, after you get ready to start this checklist, is to install lithium hydroxide canisters and to stow ORDEAL.
On panel 8, we want to turn the Floodlights to Fixed.
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: There are several potential reasons for Jack's remark here.
It is perhaps that he is tired and can't keep up with the pace, or Ken is indeed going much faster than Jack is used to.
Perhaps Ken, in his anxiety to get the procedures to the crew, speaks faster than he might do otherwise.
He is an experienced CapCom in his own right, and would have given crew procedures over the radio before.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: I'll go line at a time and wait for your verification before I go on to the next one.
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: Install LiOH canisters, stow ORDEAL, Floodlight, Fixed.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: They currently don't have lithium hydroxide canisters installed in the Command Module's ECS slots.
Should they forget to put some in, the cabin would quickly become saturated once they jettison the LM.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: The ORDEAL box will be stowed, not needed for the landing, and they will also turn on the lights.
As Jack will soon learn, even that is not as simple as it might seem.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Now we're going to take panel 5 and put the Floodlight rheostat to Flood and the LEB, that's panel 100, Floodlights are also going to Fixed.
And the purpose of this is to balance up buses A and B.
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: Panel 5; Floodlights; you want the rheostat on.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: Panel 8: EMS Main A and Main B, Open.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

These breakers would have been closed until this point.
They want to save some power by not powering the EMS until needed.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: Panel 250: CB Bat A Power Entry and Postlanding, Closed.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

transcript.speaker: Commentary

transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: Battery B Power Entry and Postlanding, Closed.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: And add Battery C Power Entry and Postlanding.
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: Battery C Power Entry and Postlanding, Closed.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

transcript.speaker: Commentary

transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: Panel 5: CB Bat Charge, Bat A, Close.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: Okay, Bat Charger, Bat B/Charge, Closed.

tokens: The same as for Battery A.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

transcript.speaker: Mattingly

transcript.speaker: Mattingly

The battery relay bus uses battery power to provide electric power to fuel cell and inverter switching controls.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: EPS Sensor Signal, Main A and Main B, Closed.

transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: I'm just about to lose you here.
Just - I've got to change Omnis.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: Could you give me that one again, please.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: On panel 275: circuit breaker Main A, Battery C, Closed.
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: Main A, Bat B, Closed on 275.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

They have been useful during the return, however, for powering temperature and pressure sensors in the SPS system, for example, to give them an idea on how cold the spacecraft has gotten during the coast home.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Panel 5: circuit breaker Essential Instrumentation Power, Main B, Closed.

tokens: Would you read that again, please?
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

These are used to provide attitude control in both automatic and manually operated modes after separation from the Service Module for the last few minutes of spaceflight and during the first moments of atmospheric entry.

tokens: Okay, now we're at EI minus 5 plus 05.
And, as far as you're concerned, Jack, the Command Module is using this for information only.
This will be the LM doing an Earth terminator AGS alignment; and at the time of 5 plus 05 is the time they'll be going ahead and releasing their ball.
And this will be covered in the LM checklist; it'll be uplinked after this one.
At EI minus 5, we perform midcourse number 7.
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

The DSKY even had a dedicated Standby button as a shortcut to this.

Ken will soon elaborate further on this procedure.

tokens: We are going to make an alignment on the Moon and on the Sun; other than that - I didn't get any - I wasn't on the headset when the general information was passed up, so if there was any more than that, I didn't get it.

tokens: The methodology was discussed briefly before, but will now be described in detail to Jack, who will be using this advice to get the IMU running again.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Let me just run through it hand-waving type for a minute, and then I'll read you the steps.
I think it will make more sense.
The scheme is that the LM can fly to a set of ball angles that will point your optics at the Moon.

Now, at any time, in any attitude that we happen to be at, if you can look in the telescope and recognize star patterns sufficient that you believe you can make an alignment all on your own, that's the place we'd like to break off and let you go ahead and take over with your own alignment.

However, we are giving you a procedure which will give you things we know you can see in the telescope without worrying about any reflections or anything of that nature, and it's not intended that this procedure has to be followed past the point where you know you've got good definition of star patterns in your telescope.

If you need to go on, we'll maneuver, take a look at the Moon, we'll take a mark there, and then we'll maneuver with the LM/FDAI angles again and take a look at the Sun, and get yourself another mark on the Sun and that will complete the alignment.
Now, the alignment scheme is that we are going to give you a set of angles that you can coarse align the ball through when the LM is pointing you at the Moon.
Now, this set of angles will agree with the REFSMMAT that you have in as a nominal REFSMMAT in your CMC.
You can coarse align to that, set a drift flag and REFSMMAT flag, and you'll have a good platform and we'll be within a couple of degrees of a good alignment.

And, if we have to, we could break off at this point and go ahead with an entry; however, in an attempt to get a good platform, we are going to have you then do a P52 option 1, and what you are going to find in the option 1 slots is the entry REFSMMAT.
You'll come up with the Noun 22 values, and we intend to go ahead and coarse align the ball to the entry REFSMMAT attitude, and then we'll go ahead and take marks through a normal P52 sequence.
So that's the general scheme that we are going to use.
If you think you have a handle on that, I will go ahead and read you the steps.
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: This is a very long readup from Ken.
The main idea, however, is to align the Command Module inertial platform based on gimbal angles derived from the known orientation of the Lunar Module.
They plan to refine the alignment taking sightings of the Moon and the Sun - using these targets eliminates their running issues with poor visibility of stars through the optics.
Should they be able to see the stars, however, the crew can proceed to do the realignment with star sightings, just like any other such realignment during a normal mission.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Well, let me ask a question.
The LM maneuvers to a set of Moon-viewing angles.
Okay, at this time, I am going to do a P52, is that right?
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: The first thing we are going to do is to coarse align the platform and get it inertial, based on the REFSMMAT that you have in the present slot inside the CMC.
We'll then go - and do a P52, option 4 - Correction, option 1.

tokens: Okay - That's after the LM gets established at the attitude.

transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: I do a P52 option 1, and when do I set the REFSMMAT flag and the drift flag?

tokens: Maybe it'll all fall out and I won't have any questions.

tokens: Verb 41 (Coarse Align CDU) Noun 20 (Present ICDU Angles) coarse aligns the IMU to angles input via the DSKY.
This will be used as basis for the upcoming P52 realignment.

tokens: And you'll put in the attitudes that you'll get from one of the PADs, and this will be for the coarse align angles when the LM is at the Moon-viewing attitude.
Then when they're all squared away, I tell you they have the right attitude; we then pick up again with a Verb 40 Noun 20, Enter.

transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: If I can define the center through the sextant, do it that way.
And if I can't, use the telescope.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: We know you're going to use the telescope on the Sun.
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: Just reminded we owe somebody a Bat B voltage.
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: Okay, another volts and amps coming up.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: And this left us in P52 with two marks, and we'll go ahead and torque the Noun 93s.
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: [Garbled] torque the Noun 93s, after the two marks.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: They will perform the fine align on the platform by taking sightings of the Earth and the Moon and marking the positions into the computer.
Afterwards, Noun 93 will drive the gimbals to hence-determined fine orientation.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: So that gives us a good platform.

Now the LM will maneuver to the jettison attitude, and the only thing of significance here is that you want to watch to make sure that the path they choose to go to the jettison attitude avoids gimbal lock on your own platform.
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: The LM maneuvers to the jettison attitude and I just monitor the maneuver and yell at Jim if he gets near a gimbal lock.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: The newly aligned platform can go into gimbal lock if the middle gimbal angle (on the CM roll axis) exceeds 90 degrees.

It is possible for this to happen when the LM maneuvers them around, since the two spacecraft have different roll axes and the safe LM maneuver may inadvertedly rotate the Command Module into the gimbal lock zone.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Okay, and then the LM will go to a Max dead band attitude hold in the jettison attitude.
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: The LM goes to Max dead band attitude hold.
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: Now we're ready to go back to work.
The next thing we'll do is to continue to powerup procedures.
Do you have any further questions on that alignment, Jack?
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: I think - I think it's all self-explanatory now.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: The one thing that everybody would like for you to understand is we certainly don't insist on using the Moon and the Earth; if you find you have good stars, why use them.
However, this alignment is more than adequate to give us a good guided entry.
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: If I get a set of good stars, do I start right out to a basic P51 and a P52 option 1?
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: They all know that an IMU orientation based on Earth-Sun marking will not be as accurate as using stars for the purpose.
However, they are aware that with the heavy constraints on their ability to maneuver as well as diffficulty spotting the stars, this should be adequate for the reentry and landing.
All they really need to do is present the CM in approximately the correct initial attitude for entry.
Once they actually enter the atmosphere, aerodynamic forces will take over and determine the CM's attitude.
However, should the opportunity present itself, Jack can also try to look for the stars and perform a more nominal calibration using them.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Now, we're ready to go to work on panel 275.
And it starts with a circuit breaker Main A...
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: I owe you some volts and amps.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: I thought maybe your meter was stuck before.
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: You ready to go back to 275?
Have a circuit breaker Main A, Bat Bus A, Closed.
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: CB Main A, Bat Bus A, Closed.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: This circuit breaker connects Battery Bus A to Main Bus A.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: And circuit breaker Main B, Bat C, Closed.
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: CB Main Baker, Bat Charlie, Closed.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: This connects Battery C to Main Bus B.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Now we're up to EI minus 1 plus 30.
And at this time, we're going to proceed with the closeout and hatch installation.
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: Closeout and hatch installation at EI minus 01:30.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: That's right, Jack, and we're going to close the LM hatch, and we'll make the dump valve in the LM hatch closed.
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: Understand you're going to close the dump valve, now on the LM hatch, right?
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: This is a change from what we had understood previously.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: I'm - Personally, I'm happy to see it, but that's good.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: That's one vote on your side.
Okay; and we're going to go ahead and do the - use the decal to perform the hatch installation, and we'll start on the hatch integrity check.
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: Per the decal, perform the hatch installation and start on the hatch integrity check.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

At what point in time should they be removed?
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: We're going to be separating the tunnel upstream of those umbilicals, and you can leave them right there.
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: John Aaron's just saying that they may be hot.
He's checking on it, so how about holding a decision on that and let me come back to you?
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: John Aaron has been working with the special team to produce these procedures, and they would be aware of such things.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: And let me tell you what we're going to do since we told you a different story this afternoon on this tunnel.
We're actually going to vent the tunnel down so that we end up with about 1½ to 2 psi absolute in the tunnel.
And this will give us a Delta-P of 3.
So we'll do our hatch integrity check at the same time we're venting the tunnel down.
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: The plan is to open the valve in the Command Module that allows them to vent the docking tunnel, and take the pressure down to be about 2 psia.
While they do this, they can also monitor the CM cabin pressure for any signs of excessive leakage due to a badly fitting top hatch.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Now let me give you some plumbing switches here.
Let's take the Surge Tank Oxygen valve to On.
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

transcript.speaker: Commentary

transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: Okay, Water And Glycol Tanks Pressure and Relief valve to Both.

transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: And Emergency Cabin Pressure valve to Both.
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: That's correct; and the Suit Demand Regs to Both.

tokens: The suit demand regulators supply O2into the spacesuit pressure circuit.

tokens: Okay, now we're going to panel 5: circuit breaker ECS Transducers, Pressure Group 1 and 2, Main A and Main B.
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: ECS Transducers, Group 1 and 2, Main A and Main B, four, to Close.

tokens: Primary ECS Glycol Pumps to 1, AC1.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: This sets up the glycol pump power supply to AC Bus 1.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Okay, then we'll finish going back to the tunnel vent, and verify the LM/CM Delta-P and make sure it's been holding pressure.

The next thing I want to read off is the EI minus 1 plus 20.
We'll go to panel 7: BMAG number 1 Power to Warm Up.
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: BMAG number 1 Power to Warm Up.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: The BMAGs needs to have power applied to them for some time before they are turned on properly, to condition the electronics to operational status.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Then at EI minus 1 plus 10, we'll perform SCS powerup per checklist, page G/2-4.
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: Perform SCS powerup per G&C checklist, page 2-4.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: This procedure from the Guidance and Navigation Checklist gives the instructions on getting the Stabilization Control System back online.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Okay, on panel 4: Suit Compressor Number 2 to AC1.

This step is to select AC Bus 1 as its power source.

The CM would then have an active attitude control system.

tokens: And your Manual Attitude switches, as you desire.
We're coming up on jettison of the LM, so that's just like separation.

tokens: And we'll do a GDC align.

tokens: On panel 8: Rotation Control Power, Direct, 1 and 2, Main A/Main B.

tokens: These are circuit breakers, Rotation Control Power, Direct, 1 and 2, Main A and Main B, Closed?
transcript.speaker: Swigert

I think we have to get both of them.
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: Okay, there's those four circuit breakers over there, Ken.
When you said panel 8, that's what I thought you were talking about.

transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: Okay, at EI minus 1 hour, we're going to be doing the LM jettison; so just prior to that, we want to do a P47.
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: Okay, say at about 1 minute prior to LM jettison, huh?
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: Yes, just something that's comfortable for you.
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: Program 47 is the Command Module thrust monitor, and will display the acceleration experienced on all three axes - X,Y,Z- on the DSKY.

transcript.speaker: Commentary

transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: I get a Go fron MSFN for this?
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: Okay, John says we can watch it.
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: Since by this point the radio should be operational, Mission Control will be able to take part in the procedure by giving their approval.

tokens: Now we're going back to panel 8, and it's Sequential, Pyro Arm, two of them, to Safe.
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: Seq Pyro Arm, two, to Safe.

transcript.speaker: Commentary

transcript.speaker: Mattingly

transcript.speaker: Mattingly

We'll have the DAP loads for you later.

tokens: Minimum Impulse will use very small bursts of RCS fire, saving fuel and keeping them steady.

tokens: And as soon as you've gotten yourself all set up with a good control mode, you want to maneuver to the entry attitude.

tokens: Maneuver to entry attitude per the PAD.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: Okay, this is just a precaution, Jack.
If we park the optics with a 90-degree shaft angle, that will line up the slits so it gives you the best airflow over that part of the heat shield and avoids a hot spot.
If you don't remember it, I don't think it makes much difference.
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: Any proturberances on the surface of the Command Module generate additional heating as they alter the airflow during the hardship of re-entry.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: And after you've got it parked wherever you want it, well, turn the Optics Power Off and stow the optics.
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: Optics Power, Off; stow the optics.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: The sextant and the telescope have detachable eyepieces that were stored in a box on the top of the optics station.
Having them come loose and hitting the crew or equipment would have been extremely dangerous.
There was precedent, too - during the reentry of Apollo 12, the DAC 16mm film camera came loose and struck Alan Bean on the head.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: The next thing we'll do is to initialize the EMS and that's on entry checklist page 2-1, step number 2.
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: Okay, perform EMS initialization per entry checklist page 2-1, step 2.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: Now we're coming up on EI minus 45 minutes, and we'll give you an entry PAD and a final state vector.
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: Okay, at minus 45, entry PAD and final state vector from MSFN.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: Final updates will be made both over the radio to be written down as a PAD, and then also by uploading Mission Control's best tracking-based position data directly into the computer.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Now we're going to perform what we call the Moon check.
Rather than go into detail, if you already know what we're doing just say so; otherwise, I'll describe the use of the Moon-attitude set.
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: You remember everybody in the past has commented on seeing Moonset just a couple minutes prior to EI, and your horizon will probably be dark at the time that you get there; you're having an early morning entry; and coming in from the west, you'll still be looking at a dark horizon.
So, just as a backup technique, just like you would track the horizon, we're going to give you an attitude which will be on the PAD so that you can look directly at the Moon.
This is an inertial attitude; it won't be changing, and you'll put the 36-degree window line on the Moon, and then just sit there and watch it.
And just prior to EI, the Earth will come up and occult it, and you will then have a good horizon check.
And I have some times, and I'll give you that later.
And if you just look at this thing now, at that point you can set the GDC or whatever you desire.
And you will have a known attitude reference.
It's just like a horizon check.
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: Okay, I maneuver to the Moon check attitude; at which time I put the 36 window line on the Moon, and just prior to EI, the Earth will occult the Moon.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: And that corresponds to the same inertial attitude that you'd have on the back of your entry card where it shows the horizon attitude several minutes prior to EI; it's the same thing.
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: Yes, that's that one at 17 minutes prior.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: The Moon will provide them with a simple visual reference to ensure their entry attitude is correct, by having it line up with a degree line painted onto their hatch window.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Okay, and we're just getting it in a little closer.
Okay, the next item on the check - checklist here is an EI minus 40; we're going to panel 7, and it's BMAG Number 2 Power to Warm Up.
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: Okay, panel 7: BMAG Number 2 Power to Warm Up.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: They will start powering up the second BMAG gyroscope.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Now, I'd like to verify the Surge Tank and Repress Packages are On.
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: This is Apollo Control at 127 hours, 31 minutes.
We've completed the shift handover in Mission Control.
A change of shift briefing is scheduled to begin in about 15 minutes at 9 pm in the main News Center Auditorium, in Building 1...
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Verify Surge Tank and Repress Package are On.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: Once the LM is gone, their sole oxygen supply will be in the surge tank and the repress package within the Command Module.

There should be ample oxygen to get them down, and even with no oxygen left in the tanks, the Command Module is rated to support them for one hour until the oxygen inside runs out and the air becomes dangerously saturated with carbon dioxide.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: On panel 5: circuit breaker Environmental - Control System; Waste Water/Urine Dump Heater, two of them, Closed.
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: Panel 5: CB ECS, Waste H2O/Urine Dump Heaters, two, to Close.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: These breakers control power to the dump line heaters.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: That's right, Jack, and what this - what we're planning to do in order to conserve power is we're going to run the suit compressor from an hour to this point at minus 40.
And we'll be watching the PCO2; if that thing's working, we'll turn it off at the earliest time that we have a good cabin, and then we'll just leave it off.
And in the event that the PCO2gauge isn't working properly or something like that, well, we'll just go ahead and run it for this time frame.
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: The suit compressor consumes 2 amps of power, and would be a drain on their battery power supply.
Should the air show signs of becoming dangerously saturated with carbon dioxide, they will be able to spare power for the compressor, however.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Sequential, Logic, that's two of them, to On, up.
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: EI minus 30; Seq, Logic, two, On.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: The Sequential Logic system will be used during reentry to release their various parachutes.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Okay, in panel 7: the BMAG number 2 Power to On.
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: Okay, we - you only gave that 10 minutes for the warmup, huh?
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: All right, and FDAI Power to Both.
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: This powers the two FDAI 8-balls on the Main Display Console.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: And we want to activate the primary evaporator.
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: All this time, Ken, what we've been getting our cooling?
Right now, I think we have the radiators at Pull To Bypass.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: We started out that way, and we turned the glycol pumps on, and we're just circulating it internally.
And we're counting on the Command Module being a heat sink; it's pretty cold soaked, and it looks like this is good enough.
You got some relatively low heat loads...
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

Ken doesn't think it will be a problem, with the relatively small amount of heat generated, plus the Command Module being very cold and taking time to warm up.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: ...and we'll keep watching the temps, and if we need it, why we'll start the evaporator early.
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: Do you believe it's cold up there?
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: Yes, and we used to call the Command Module the bedroom and it's now the refrigerator.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: Okay, just don't call it the freezer.
At about EI minus 19, we get back into the standard entry checklist on page 2-2, where it starts with P61.
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: Okay, at EI minus 19, we get back into the entry checklist at page 2-2, where we start entering P61.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: On the bottom of page 2-2, they are able to start using the normal entry procedures checklists as basis for their actions, with a few modifications to the timeline and events.
Here they would start Program 61 in the computer, to begin the reentry calculations.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Now that's the - those are the timeline notes I had for you.
I hate to tell you this, but I also have a couple of corrections to make to the entry checklist.
They aren't corrections, they just are pieces of the entry checklist which will correspond to what I've written - already read to you.
And I'm ready to go on that whenever you are.
And Vance tells me you can get into the descent water for drinking now.
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: We can get into the descent water for drinking, huh?
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: Jim would like to know whether the descent water will be good until LM jettison?
transcript.speaker: Swigert

transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: I'm sorry we cut each other out; say again.
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: I was going to say, understand we're going to go on the ascent tanks at 128:30.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: That's right; based on the current rate, that's what they predict and you can go ahead and drink the water.

transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: (Laughter) I hope it stays that way.
Okay, Jack, we're starting on page 1-1.
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: Okay, down in the middle of the page on line 9, we're just going to strike out the "Activate VHF for Comm checks," and line 10, "Verify the DSE." We're going to leave the DSE Off, and that's to save power, as is the VHF activation.
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: At minus 2 hours, there's a title "Logic sequence" check; and we're deleting that entire sequence; that's included in what I read up to you.
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: Okay, delete the logic sequence check at minus 2 hours there.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: That means a maneuver to Supercirc entry attitude.
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

The maneuver at the end of the page is unnecessary info on the checklist, since they've been given a new procedure.

tokens: All right, on line 29 on page 1-3, we're going to put in a time of minus 00:55.

tokens: And, after we go EMS Mode to Standby, like for you to add "Circuit breaker EMS, two, to Open." [Pause.]
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

We're deleting that for power reasons.
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Okay, we're way ahead of you.

tokens: On the top of the next page where it says "Secondary water EVAP," just keep in mind we don't plan to use the secondary evaporators unless the primary fails.
On line 35 on page 1-5, next to "Command Module RCS preheat," the time is now "Minus 06:30." [Pause.]
transcript.speaker: Mattingly

tokens: Okay, "minus 06:30," and the voltage reading 3.9 still is good, right?

tokens: All right, still on page 1-5, line 37; the time is now "Minus 06:10." [Pause.]

tokens: And now let's go to page 1-6.

And about one-third of the way down, it shows "Panel 275 CB Main A, Bat C, Closed," we want to strike off that line, the next two lines, and we're going to strike out "Main A, Bat C, Closed; Main B, Bat C, Closed;" and "DC Indicator, Main B."
